explanation.text: Todd enjoys groceries, math problems, and neat calculations, while Amy is absorbed in a book and barely notices her mother enter. These contrasting details show that the siblings have different interests.

The three move slowly into the kitchen, and the narrator imagines their tails tucked between their legs. That guilty, reluctant movement suggests they would rather avoid facing the kitchen and their mother.

The family has only sixty minutes for cleaning and cooking, and their father even asks whether a thirty-minute recipe can be shortened to fifteen. Therefore, a sixty-minute recipe would take too long.

Their father asks how the food pyramid works, and Todd explains its food groups while Amy clarifies what their mother probably expects. This shows the father depends on his children's school knowledge to plan the meal.
Paragraph 12 explains the food pyramid's wide grain base, small fats-and-sugars tip, and other food groups. The illustration visually organizes those same groups and proportions.
Paragraph 8 gives the mother's instructions to clean, put away groceries, and prepare a balanced meal. Paragraph 28 shows the completed result when the father sends Amy to call her mother to dinner.

The mother's demanding task forces the family to solve problems, use the food pyramid, select a recipe, and prepare a full meal. Because of the challenge, they learn practical cooking and planning skills.

The opening describes Clara as shy around strangers, unable to speak before a crowded classroom, and physically weakened by the experience. These behaviors therefore establish how strongly other people affected her.
Clara became pale and lost weight at school, so her parents, teachers, and doctor met to discuss her condition. Their concern for her health led them to decide she should return home.

Clara's parents try to restrict her adventurous behavior by forbidding ice skating, but she secretly persuades her cousins to teach her. In context, “rein Clara in” means to restrain her determination to explore and learn.
Clara explores streams, climbs onto mill poles, secretly learns to skate despite repeated falls, and practices horseback riding. These energetic risks show that she is active and adventurous.

Paragraph 9 identifies Clara's great-aunt as a midwife who treated illnesses, then explains Clara's satisfaction from nursing David. This connects Clara's growing desire to care for others with a family example of service.
Clara spends two years nursing David and discovers how useful and satisfied the work makes her feel. Because of that experience, she later seeks meaningful ways to help sick and struggling families.
Clara's lasting memory of how satisfying it felt to nurse David explains her later search for people who needed help. That motivation is central to a summary of her development into a caregiver.
After calmly reviewing fundamentals, Gaters increases the intensity by sharply criticizing sloppy play, even during an easy win. Thus, “take it up a notch” means becoming more forceful.
Gaters openly tells players when their play is sloppy and insists on fundamentals even when they are winning. Such difficult honesty shows that she holds her team to demanding standards.

The article says Pondexter starred at Rutgers and that Gaters helped many other players reach college, one of her proudest achievements. This evidence shows how Gaters can take players beyond high school.

Gaters checks grades, calls parents about skipped classes, buys needed clothing, and sometimes takes players out socially. These actions show that her tough love extends to every part of her players' lives.

Photographs show Gaters honored at the White House, inducted into the Women's Basketball Hall of Fame, and recognized with a lifetime achievement award. These honors demonstrate appreciation for her contributions to girls basketball.

When Gaters mentions retirement, Howard playfully dismisses the idea and says the coach would continue forever if possible. That reaction suggests Gaters remains deeply devoted to and enthusiastic about her work.

Gaters pushes players to finish school, support themselves and their families, attend college, and meet needs off the court. People respect her because she teaches responsibility and life success, not only basketball.

An earthquake shakes the ground with great force; similarly, loud volume violently shakes the membrane supporting the ear's hair cells. The simile shows how strongly sound can physically affect the ear.
Paragraphs 4 and 5 explain how high-decibel sounds shake the ear's membrane and permanently break or uproot hair cells. Paragraph 6 then gives safe-volume rules that can prevent this damage.
Paragraph 4 claims that high-volume MP3 use can damage the ear's delicate hair cells. Yahaira's ringing ears after listening to loud music provide a concrete symptom supporting that claim.

Modern music companies use compression to raise recordings' overall volume, so a new song sounds louder without changing the player's setting. Older recordings from the 1980s and 1990s were not controlled this way.
Paragraph 5 traces the damage directly: a loud sound shakes the membrane, then breaks or uproots hair cells that cannot regrow. This mechanism most clearly explains how noise harms hearing.

Paragraph 13 describes sound moving from the auditory canal to the eardrum, ossicles, cochlea, auditory nerve, and finally the brain. The labeled drawing therefore helps readers follow that order through the ear.
The article's main warning is that loud music can permanently destroy hair cells and cause hearing loss. Choosing a lower volume therefore directly addresses that danger and protects hearing.
